items.stem: A 68-year-old man with long-standing hypertension presents with progressive dyspnea on exertion, orthopnea, and bilateral ankle edema. Exam reveals an S3 gallop, elevated JVP, and bibasilar crackles. BNP is 890 pg/mL. Echocardiography shows an LVEF of 30% with global hypokinesis. Which initial pharmacologic regimen is most appropriate?
options: Furosemide alone until euvolemia, then reassess
ACE inhibitor, beta-blocker, and loop diuretic
items.explanation: Guideline-directed medical therapy for HFrEF includes an ACE inhibitor (or ARB/ARNI), evidence-based beta-blocker, and diuretic for congestion. CCBs are not first-line; inotropes are reserved for cardiogenic shock.
items.reference: Management: "Guideline-directed medical therapy for heart failure with reduced ejection fraction includes inhibitors of the renin-angiotensin system, beta blockers, and mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists."

items.stem: The same patient remains on optimal GDMT but continues NYHA class III symptoms with LVEF 28%. He is in sinus rhythm with a QRS duration of 150 ms and LBBB morphology. Which additional intervention has the strongest evidence for mortality benefit?
options: Catheter ablation of the AV node
items.explanation: CRT improves symptoms, reduces hospitalizations, and decreases mortality in selected patients with HFrEF, sinus rhythm, LBBB, and QRS ≥150 ms despite GDMT.
items.reference: Device Therapy: "Cardiac resynchronization therapy is recommended for patients with LVEF ≤35%, sinus rhythm, left bundle branch block with QRS duration ≥150 ms, and persistent symptoms despite GDMT."
